problem_summary: 2012 MCM Problem B ("The Big Long River"), a discrete optimization/scheduling problem. A 225-mile wilderness river is accessible only at First Launch and Final Exit, with Y campsites in between. Trips last 6-18 nights using either oar rafts (4 mph) or motor boats (8 mph); two boats may not share a campsite on the same night, and no boat may travel upstream. The task is to design a schedule that maximizes the number of trips per season (the river's "carrying capacity") while spreading trips so the river is fairly utilized and "wilderness" contact between boats is minimized. This team reframes it as a deterministic, priority-based discrete-event scheduling simulation over a campsite-by-night grid, fully implemented in Java, then sweeps parameters with Monte Carlo runs to find the carrying capacity and an efficiency metric.
models_used: Discrete grid (matrix) state model: schedule represented as a 2D array Boat[NUM_DAYS][numSites] (rows = campsites, columns = nights), so a schedule is literally a table of which boat occupies which site on which night
Discrete-event greedy simulation with a priority queue: day-by-day advance of all boats, each day's moves resolved in priority order via Java PriorityQueue (insert/findMin/deleteMin)
Priority rule (the core 'model'): priority = fewest days remaining first; tie-break gives oar boats priority over motor boats (encoded in Boat.compareTo)

Pacing / target-speed movement formula: a boat's desired advance per day is ceil((Y - S_k)/d_k) + m_C campsites, bounded above by max sites/day floor(v_k*h*(Y+1)/225); m_C is a tunable 'movement constant' that makes boats move a bit faster than strictly needed

Conflict-resolution by role-swapping: when a higher-priority boat wants an occupied site and cannot find an open site upstream of its previous position, boats 'swap roles' recursively (since no boat can go backward), guaranteeing termination because at least one site was vacated upstream

First-come-first-served admission queue (Java LinkedList as a deque): addBoats() injects waiting trips into reachable empty top-of-river sites each day; treated as an effectively infinite request queue
Rescheduling operator: late trips are erased from the grid and pushed back to the front of the queue (reschedule())
Closed-form carrying-capacity / efficiency model: M = 182*Y / L_bar (max trips), % efficiency = n*L_bar / (182*Y) * 100% — campsite-night utilization
Closed-form 'magic numbers' theory: integer-floor analysis of max-distance formulas D_M=floor(8h(Y+1)/225), D_O=floor(4h(Y+1)/225); magic when D_M(Y) != D_M(Y-1) AND D_M(Y) = 2*D_O(Y)

Monte Carlo experimentation: testUniform/testGaussian generate ~2000 random trip requests, run 1000 iterations x 10 points, averaging trips/crossovers/reschedules under uniform and Gaussian (truncated) trip-length distributions
key_techniques: Reframing an optimization problem as a constructive simulation rather than an LP/IP: instead of solving a formal program, they build a good schedule greedily and then bound how good it could be with the M=182Y/L_bar ceiling, reporting the gap (98.8% of optimal)

Encoding the entire physics/rules into a clean two-class object model (Boat, River) with one comparator that IS the scheduling policy — policy lives in Boat.compareTo, making the priority rule auditable in ~6 lines

Deriving the per-day max advance as a discrete quantity (campsites/day = floor(speed*h*(Y+1)/225)), which converts continuous mph into integer grid steps and is the hinge for the magic-number discovery

A 'pace yourself' insight: targeting average required speed plus a small constant m_C beat the naive 'go as far as possible' heuristic, raising trips ~10.7% (557.9 -> 617.8) and cutting reschedules from 0.793 to 0.166
Tuning m_C as a percentage of max travel distance (best ~8-15%, scaling with site count) rather than a fixed value
Recursive role-swap conflict resolution that respects the no-upstream constraint and is provably terminating

Independent verification harness: checkSchedule() audits the produced schedule for late/early/'speeding' (constraint-violating) boats, and countCrossovers() independently counts boat passings — i.e., they tested their own simulator
Sensitivity sweeps treated as step-functions: identifying that efficiency is flat between magic numbers and drops ~30-33% at 'black magic' thresholds (e.g., 8.7 vs 8.8 hours, 258 vs 257 miles)

Separating the two operating regimes (first-come-first-served requests vs pre-scheduled trips) and showing the same engine serves both, with 'run thousands of random schedules and keep the best' as the pre-scheduling strategy
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on four axes. (1) It actually answers the question asked: it computes the river's carrying capacity (essentially Y, the number of campsites, with the schedule grid nearly full) and gives the manager an operational tool, not just a number. (2) It quantifies optimality honestly — rather than claiming "our schedule is good," it derives a theoretical ceiling M=182Y/L_bar and reports being within 98.8% of it, which is exactly the kind of self-grading judges reward. (3) The 'magic numbers' discovery is the memorable, original nugget every winning paper needs: a non-obvious periodic (period-7, then period-11) structure in reschedules vs site count, then explained from first principles via the floor functions and even/odd parity of motor-vs-oar speeds. It looks like genuine discovery, not curve-fitting. (4) Engineering credibility: complete, commented, runnable Java with a real priority queue, plus a self-verification harness (checkSchedule/countCrossovers) and 1000-iteration Monte Carlo averaging — the work is clearly reproducible and the team clearly understood discrete data structures. The writing is also disciplined: a strong standalone Summary, a manager Memo, explicit goals, and a clean strengths/weaknesses section that volunteers the model's flaws.
section_highlights: Summary (lines 1-15): three crisp paragraphs that name the priority rule, the M=182Y/L_bar capacity ceiling, the 98.8% result, AND the magic-number formula — a reader knows the whole contribution before page 2. Memo (17-23): translates magic numbers into a one-sentence operating instruction for the river manager ("plug in Y, pick an h that is a magic number, run the algorithm"). Assumptions (37-44): six numbered, individually justified assumptions, each tied to why it makes the discrete model clean (equal spacing -> 225/(Y+1); Y>=18 so 6-night oar trips can finish). The Model + General Algorithm (66-271): clean Boat/River decomposition, the schedule-as-grid representation with an explicit day-vs-night off-by-one warning, and the full annotated algorithm walked through as four cases (best / nearly-best / open-site-upstream / worst-case role swap) with Figures 1-2 illustrating the swap. Optimization (373-377): the m_C derivation with before/after numbers. Magic Numbers (379-420): the standout section — scatterplot, worked floor-function arithmetic for Y=34 vs 35, the parity caveat (only even motor distances trigger it), and a clean general formula. Efficiency (422-434): explicit utilization metric and the parameter set giving 659.4 trips (~98.8%). Error/Sensitivity (436-463): step-function behavior, ratio sweep (Fig 4), uniform-vs-Gaussian comparison, linear scaling in season length and site count. Appendix: complete Boat.java/River.java including the verification and Monte Carlo drivers.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Greedy, not optimal: it is a constructive heuristic with no optimality proof; the 98.8% is measured against their own derived ceiling, not a solved IP, so 'optimal' is asserted by proximity, not established
FCFS launch ordering is admitted as a weakness — they tried smarter orderings and all did worse, but never explain why, leaving real performance on the table (their own 'future work')

Rescheduling by full erase-and-requeue is crude; they acknowledge a rollback approach would be cleaner but did not implement it. The reschedule() also pushes to the FRONT of the queue, which can starve other waiting trips

The efficiency metric is gamed by the model's own design choice: lines 27-31 force boats to sit at the last site until their requested length elapses, so trips never finish 'early' — meaning the grid is artificially kept full and the 98.8% utilization partly reflects this padding rather than genuine throughput

Magic-number generality is overstated: the team themselves note the period-11 pattern breaks down at high site counts and efficiency barely changes at magic numbers there, so the phenomenon is real but narrower than the triumphant framing suggests

Heavy hard-coding of constants (RIVER_LENGTH=225, NUM_DAYS=183, MOVEMENT_CONSTANT=4, speeds 80/40 mi/day) couples the code tightly to one scenario; the +4 in getPreferredNextSite is hard-coded even though the paper discusses m_C as 8-15% of max distance, a mismatch between prose and code

Distribution modeling is shallow — trip lengths drawn uniformly or from ad hoc truncated Gaussians (means 13/14, sd 3/2) with little empirical grounding beyond one rafting-company FAQ; no demand modeling, pricing, or seasonality

Crossover minimization is claimed as 'smallest possible' by an informal argument (no boat overtakes one with fewer days left) but is never optimized or proven minimal; countCrossovers() exists but crossovers are never reported as a tuned objective

Code shown in the appendix has visible transcription/OCR issues (e.g., a comment 'add a point to our score tripCount++' where the increment is commented out), so the exact runnable fidelity is uncertain
reusable_patterns: State-as-grid: model a scheduling/packing problem as a resource x time matrix and let 'schedule quality' = fill rate of that matrix — gives you a free, intuitive efficiency metric (used cells / total cells)
Derive a theoretical ceiling, then report % of ceiling: M = capacity*time / average-demand-size, and grade your heuristic against it (here 182Y/L_bar). Judges love a self-computed optimality gap
Put the policy in a comparator: encode the entire scheduling rule as one Comparable.compareTo so the decision logic is small, testable, and swappable; drive execution with a standard PriorityQueue

Pace-to-deadline movement: target = ceil(remaining_work / remaining_time) + small_slack, capped by a physical max — a clean, generalizable greedy heuristic for any 'must finish on time while sharing resources' problem

Find the discrete 'magic number' structure: when continuous parameters pass through floor()/ceil() to become integer capacities, sweep them and look for periodicity or step jumps, then explain the steps from the floor-function arithmetic and parity. A reusable way to turn a parameter sweep into a publishable insight

Build a self-verification harness: write an independent checker (checkSchedule, countCrossovers) that audits your simulator's output for constraint violations — cheap insurance and strong evidence of rigor

Monte Carlo the demand: generate thousands of random instances under multiple distributions (uniform AND Gaussian), average over many runs, and report variance/insensitivity as your sensitivity analysis
Step-function sensitivity framing: present robustness as 'flat between thresholds, cliffs at thresholds' rather than smooth derivatives when the model is discrete

Dual deliverable: a technical body plus a one-page manager Memo that compresses the model into a single operating instruction — packaging that signals you solved the real-world problem, not just the math
